blocks.md: A dfn is a direct function written in braces. Inside the braces, `⍵` is the right argument and `⍺` is the left argument. The last executed expression is the result.
A dfn that uses only `⍵` is monadic. A dfn that uses `⍺` and `⍵` is dyadic. Assign it with `←`, then call it like any other function.
The argument can be a whole array. If the body uses scalar functions, they apply across that array. `dbl` doubled every item without a loop because `×` already knows how to act on arrays.

blocks.prompt: Define `sq`, a function that squares its argument. It will be tested on 12 and should give 144.
blocks.hint: inside the dfn, multiply ⍵ by itself

blocks.prompt: Define `mean` as sum divided by count. It will be tested on `10 20 30 40` and should give 25.
blocks.hint: sum with +/⍵; count with ≢⍵; divide the two

blocks.prompt: Define dyadic `pct`, where `A pct B` returns `A` as a percentage of `B`. It will be tested as `30 pct 200` and should give 15.
blocks.hint: use ⍺ for the left argument and ⍵ for the right argument

blocks.prompt: Define a function that sums the first `⍵` integers. It will be tested on 10 and should give 55.
blocks.hint: build the range with ⍳⍵, then sum it with +/

blocks.prompt: Define `evens`, a function that keeps only the even items of its argument. It will be tested on `⍳10`.
blocks.hint: build an evenness mask from ⍵, then compress ⍵
